item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh boy I love all of this delicious M E T A!

A happy +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, hey. Just thought you might like to know that this piece of meta f-ery (which I upvoted) has been translated to French.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I can see why people wouldn't feel so hot on this article given the recent surge in overly-cutesy and reference-y/self-aware meta articles, but I don't think this one is to blame for any of those, especially considering how contentious and purposefully over-the-top it was supposed to be in the first place (you know, to the point where it'd be safe to assume people wouldn't be copping its style over a year since). It's one of the better kinds of these sorts of articles anyway; I'm not a fan but it actually does do a number of clever stuff while still keeping itself relatively tame and accessible.

item.title: Re: +💯
item.content:encoded: Started at the bottom now we here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple things here about your feedback that I either didn't understand, didn't make sense, or didn't really have a solid foundation to begin with, and thus I'm having a hard time drawing anything constructive from.

Show Commentary
Hide Commentary

I don't like Skips that are canon-specific

This pretty much precludes a huge chunk of the site then? I mean, there is no canon and all, but nothing in the article hardlines it into one specific canon. In fact, Overmeta is spread across multiple canons. The point of the article is that they, combined with out of universe author efforts, have collapsed the possible canons down so much, that the fabric of this particular reality is bulging around popular author avatars, and causing havoc. There's nothing terribly canon-specific here to begin with.

or this recent fad of being excessively meta.

I've seen this complaint a lot in other locations, and I'm glad its appeared here so I can respond to it. Series IV, when all is said and done, will have a total of 1000 articles. Currently, there are maybe, maybe 20-30 articles that are meta among them. Of those, 3500 and 3999 are probably the most meta. To call it a "fad" or "excessive" is misleading, and somewhat disingenuous, this article right here is likely, the most extreme meta article on the site at this moment in time.

I can deal with a Kitchen sink approach, I'm pretty "gonzo over grimdark, let's ride 682 like Secretariat and hire the parahumans" but the Foundationverse's fourth wall shouldn't be broken in an SCP.

I'm not really sure what the majority of this sentence is illustrating? Are you saying you prefer the OTT, unprofessional behavior of early foundation writing and Author Avatars, and that you would rather see more of that without fourth wall breaks? Cause we have a canon for it on site. Its called lolfoundation. You should probably go check it out. That's besides the point though, none of that contributes in a constructive way that I or Rimple could use to improve the article, so I'm not sure why you mentioned it? Maybe you were comparing the behavior of Roget in the article to that? But that's not what's going on here with Roget's entries, its rather Roget is being faced with absurd situations, and reacting in a natural, terrified fashion as these things keep occurring.

As for the comment on fourth wall breaks, there are plenty of articles that break the fourth wall, albeit in different, subtle ways, Swanns Proposal does it, 3999 does it, and there are plenty of other examples that I won't take the time to list out here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted, hard.

I don't like Skips that are canon-specific, or this recent fad of being excessively meta. I can deal with a Kitchen sink approach, I'm pretty "gonzo over grimdark, let's ride 682 like Secretariat and hire the parahumans" but the Foundationverse's fourth wall shouldn't be broken in an SCP. 3999 is pushing it, this is just too far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: my head hurts and I need to lie down for a while and close my eyes. I think that means the pataphysical bullshit is working. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Ow.

I'm a sucker for meta. Upvoted. I need an aspirin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one. I'm admittedly a sucker for meta stuff, but I don't think that being meta can carry a skip on its own. For example, I'm not really a fan of Swann's 001 proposal. I don't find it particularly interesting to have the characters say "we're all fictional" as a reveal.

3500 doesn't use "being meta" as a reveal, or even really as the main draw. The assumption that the Foundation knows about us is the cost of entry. The benefit of doing it this way is that it runs with it. Characters simply knowing they're fictional isn't nearly as interesting as what they do with that knowledge. Their attempts to manipulate their own story from the inside are what really sells 3500 to me. Weaving elements of the SCP Foundation as a work of fiction — or as a website — into the universe and allowing characters to react is what makes it special. I couldn't help but grin when a "data hyperposition" let them predict that the floodgates of series IV would be opening up because the idea that every part of this project, not just whatever makes it into a skip or a tale, has a measurable effect on the narrative layer the Foundation exists in is an incredibly compelling one. Managing to break their own canon via in-universe efforts born from real life policy is exactly the kind of metafiction I want to see. The fact "there is no canon" is used in-universe to prompt a literal author's saving throw is honestly fucking genius. Metafiction as a tool the characters use is an angle I rarely see and it's used to create effect here.

This was always going to be a divisive one, but that very fact is used expertly in the text: If you don't like 3500, you're more than welcome to not incorporate it into your headcanon. From the perspective of the characters, all the better.

I'm going to have to think on it but this might be my favourite entry on the site. Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an inspired idea, but I have to echo Buttfranklin's criticisms. There is too much meta on this site, to the point where it is starting to detract from the meaning of our narratives. It was clever when S. Andrew Swann did it and there are a few standout examples like SCP-3999, but this gimmick is far past its shelf life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love meta stuff, but honestly I have no idea what I just read. And the in-universe-meta part.. I honestly don't know what to say.

Nonvoting for now, because I might just be missing something.

EDIT: Ok, now I think I get it (Thanks /r/SCPDeclassified). I like the overall idea and it was executed pretty well, but now that I get it, I actually dislike it even more. The meta feels like you're screaming at the reader "HAVE YOU NOTICED IT'S META YET" all throughout the article and some parts are almost incomprehensible if you don't read them 3 times over.

If there was anything as "too much meta," it'd probably be this. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know, I had trouble finding this article again because I saw it once and thought "Huh, I gotta read that one later." And then I couldn't find it :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aw man, they changed the title :-(

In all seriousness, I'm novoting. I'm not the biggest fan of meta, and I don't like the way it was executed. I liked the logs, however, which saves this from a downvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I opened this and +1'd it just off the title, expecting some delicious meta mindfucks. This is about as meta as I'm willing to upvote, but it was well done and I do like pushing boundaries, so, good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, just for the logs detailing Dr Roget's tortuous descent into insanity. Ugh, maybe I need a therapist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I agree that the amount of meta-wankery is annoying, I disagree with the notion of that this is 'meta for meta's' sake. The meta-ness is meant to help facilitate the story of how the Foundation is trying to create a consistent timeline through the Wikiwalk and the link. Sure, you can say that the meta-ness is annoying, but I'd say that it does have a purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. It was clever, and the meta aspects are fun if you know much about Roget, and his prolificity.

I'm not sure I can agree with the latter half, as that's an objective statement about something subjective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the fuck did I just read. Meta for meta's sake is wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not entirely sure on what in name of Jesus's Cheese is this, but this is amusing.

+1 for potential to be really hilarious, although seriously confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think this is a very good SCP. Taken separately, the metaRoget anomaly narrative thread has some ok build-up but is just kind of random, and wouldn't stand up without context. (For another old and deceptive rule of thumb, if this is someone's first SCP, they very likely won't get it in the slightest.) For the other thread, the whole pataphysics canon (outside of the original one with the Radiohead album) is arch and shaggy and just kind of a (fun) fucking mess, and this article is no exception. Possibly more so, since it's pinned so hard on actual authors.

I say a fucking mess because this level of meta is inherently narratively unstable. The 'verse isn't real and we are, and nothing will change that. The clever footwork here (and it is clever) doesn't (and I would argue can't) do anything to fix that instability and build a more solid/believable narrative, it just dances around and chucks jargon and crosslinks to lampshade obvious plotholes.

I still upvoted, because it was a fun read, and I had a simile on my face during the last note, but I think I'll also be happy to see the back of these as SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I only like four things: Meta-humour, repeating myself, the SCP Foundation, and meta-humour.

Good work making me upvote what I initially thought was an unsalvageable idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what the fuck did i just read and why can't i stop shrieking

(very emphatic +1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the meaning is that while these characters start their "Flat hozion "action(WHy they start this? Is that because the different canons make them difficult to live blabla, so they try to make it united? ),

They push all the canon too tight, thus resulting all these canon crushed, and this is a crisis that all narrative could break in 16 mouths?

Why they have to talk with me? what does MY opinion mean for them?Well I want them destroyedhahahha

item.title: An Actual Serious Comment From Me
item.content:encoded: In all seriousness, I loved S. Andrew Swan's proposal, and seeing it used here is refreshing. I wish more people would reference it, so much so that when I get an author page, I'm gonna name it swn-001-S88

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I'll ever get tired of skips that explain a real-world writing pattern or convention, but I didn't think anyone would try and explain the absence of a single universe in-universe. "It's canon that there are lots of different canons and timelines, and it's actually good that the site has so many because one canon couldn't support so many canons, so please write more canons" is so postmodern that I can't even wrap my head around an opinion. All I have is a reaction, which was positive.

+1

Still, it kind of feels like the website is getting closer to a "where can we even go from here?" point. Between this and SCP-3999 I'm seeing a focus on getting more meta in increasingly fundamental ways and that feels like an easy way to forget how to just enjoy the fiction. That's just me though, clearly other people are still writing "normal" skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As you can tell by the comments, it's a very controversial and divisive article. Author Avatars are fine, as long as they aren't inserted into everything and Mary Sueish, the issue here is the meta-wankery for most people and not the author avatars themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But are they cool yet?

Because that's the real question about this alleged anomalous selfie with a crayon mustache.

(I liked this article but hot damn I had to make the joke.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So are author avatars acceptable again? As great as this was, I thought it was the sort of thing that was frowned upon.

+1, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm in the +1 crowd. This is kinda like the logical limit to the metanarrative pseudo-shenanigans; a nice tie-the-concept-off-in-a-bow thing, IMO, rather than pushing it "too far." But only just.

*shamelessly blasts that one song from Scarface at max volume*

item.title: You know what you have to do now
item.content:encoded: All that's left is to make Roget into a 001 proposal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I pronounce it Roe-jay with a soft j. Might just be my French lessons talking, but I will stand by my pronunciation of it until I die.

Alternatively, Rogay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's because I'm new, but don't understand this article; I know it's meta, but I think it relies way too much on the meta. If I was even newer and this was my first article, never knowing who Roget or Overmeta was, I'd be even more lost. Which is why I'm downvoting. It can't really stand alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get where you guys are going with the metaphor - you're just saying it's wanky and self-referential, which is fair - but I hugely disagree with the idea that a moustache-pencilled image of a dear friend wouldn't count as art, or the even more ridiculous idea that it would damage the reputation of art itself.

There's intimacy to the notion, as there is intimacy to a literal self-portrait of one's navel, that both of you are entirely skipping over. You share with the world this person who is close to you, yet you give it a twist - a barrier between the friend you know and the greater world, a tongue-in-cheek disguise reminiscent of a hundred history textbooks. In a very literal way, you are putting yourself in that gap, showing your support and defense of a friend, while also reflecting how you can often see good friends in each other - the shared tics and laughs and jokes they share.

For that matter, why shouldn't art be personal, especially if there's an audience that enjoys it for what it is? Just because you don't see value in something doesn't mean it doesn't have it. It's totally cool to dislike this article, but you don't need to go shitting on other hypothetical art to demonstrate that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tbf, Rimple has no future plans, and neither do, to continue this line of "metafictional fuckery", given the high number of meta articles already present within Series IV, and the realization that many of the things Operation Overmeta wanted to do were already accomplished through 3999.

As for being tiresome to read, that's a fair and level criticism, if there was anything in particular that bothered you, feel free to let either of us know, in chat or here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Operation Overmeta was a funny userpage gimmick, but seeing this level of metafictional fuckery implemented in SCP format far exceeds my tolerance. It's just such an incredibly tiresome article to read.

I don't think we're in danger of disappearing up our own asses with meta articles, but that doesn't make it worthwhile to poke around down there. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I happen to find this entire comment chain hilarious, despite it's disparaging nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, fine, it's like taking *a photo of your friend*, pencilling on a moustache, and earnestly submitting it to an art gallery.

I dunno about that. There was an installation in a gallery titled The Lights Going On And Off. It was an empty room where the lights did just that.

It won the Turner Prize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, fine, it's like taking *a photo of your friend*, pencilling on a moustache, and earnestly submitting it to an art gallery.

Just because it's fun and cool for you and your buddies, doesn't mean the rest of the world will like it — on the contrary, the larger audience would probably get turned off by its wilful inaccessibility to people outside the artist's circle, negatively colouring their view of that art gallery as a result. It may also promote the idea amongst artists who submit work to the gallery that painting an immaculate portrait of your own navel (or your friend's) is artistically valuable and to be celebrated, audience be damned.

That's my reading of the metaphor, anyhow. And I think it's apt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would be an accurate comparison… if Roget had written this. Which they didn't. This was written by two other people on the site who admire the work that Roget has done.

So, please, explain how arrived at the conclusion that this is like a selfie with a mustache on it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like taking a selfie, penciling on a mustache, and earnestly submitting it to an art gallery

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the technical jargon is a slog, this was still a fun read all in all. Seeing more Pataphysics just makes me happy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I can ever support references to actual users in a mainlist article, profile picture and user link and all, with a well constructed canon or not. Moreover, the way this article would appear to any new reader or casual reader that doesn't know about site membership— it's an impression I don't want to be given out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I couldn't make sense of it either. I thought I was just dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was very much expecting this to be controversial, even more so after I posted the first time and was met with a lot of flack for it relying too much on Rogets own articles. Arguably, this is far better, and much better executed than the previous iteration, as indicated by the mixed controversial response, and the overall number of votes.

I think it's important that you and others have responded in both negative and positive manners. It tells us where the line is with the readers, and sparks discussion on where the line is for varying people when it comes to meta content. I knew going in, and so did Rimple, that this would very much be one of those SCPs that drew controversey, and would generate heated debates about how far is too far, and we're OK with that as long as people read the piece and get something out of it.

We're both also aware of the issues that the article has outside of the overly meta content, for example, we're both aware that the jargon is rather heavy. It's why the note is there at the end, without the Jargon, to give you a tongue in cheek explanation of what those first two collapsibles really mean. I'm also aware that the table is a virtual brick wall of text. I have some plans to fiddle with it in the future, make it more readable, put in line breaks or something, but honestly, I considered reformatting before we posted, and I just couldn't find a way to properly reformat. It just disrupted the miniature narratives about Rogets slow descent into madness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that Roget upvoted this makes me snicker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you had fun writing this, and clearly it makes sense and is funny to various people who are more involved the wiki than me - as they said in this talkpage - but to me, a simple casual reader, this doesn't make a goddamn bit of sense, and what does make sense is kind of… zany and masturbatory.

If this had been a tale, or if it had been a -J, then maybe it would have worked better (maybe) - but the SCP series itself doesn't seem like the right place for it. SCPs can have humour (god knows I believe that) and SCPs can do weird fourth wall shit but there's knocking on the fourth wall and then there's rubbing your butt on the fourth wall, pointing at your butt, and saying "this is a Keter butt".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't speak for Bleep, but yeah, I was totally expecting it. It's OK for people not to like our stuff so long as there are people who DO enjoy it. I consider it this way - if you're going to make a piece which some people are gonna dislike for the intent alone, may as well lean all the way into it so that the people who DO like it will get the most enjoyment out of it.

I think I got away with it more in ÓverMeta because of the more comedic tone, and because it was an author page, so people could enjoy it without worrying about it affecting canon (exactly the sort of paradigm Penelope tries to emulate at the end of this, in a number of ways).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not the delivery at all! The delivery was basically perfect. It's really well written, and structured very clearly, as long as you can barrel through the vocabulary. All of the crosslinks were useful and strengthened the narrative; (un)fortunately, I'm sufficiently obsessed with this site that I appreciated the vast majority of them. If we had never seen a meta article on this site, I would absolutely love this.

However, we have seen plenty of meta articles here, and this one takes meta to its absolute, navel-gazing-with-a-microscope extreme. As a result, I don't see this as cheekily transgressive, I see it as depraved, self-indulgent blasphemy.

*Sigh* I know this sounds melodramatic, and I am hamming it up a bit to indicate that I'm at least self-aware in how sanctimonious I'm being. But I imagine both of you knew from the outset that this would be a controversial piece, so I hope you allowed for the possibility of one or two very negative reactions like this one. (Although maybe not to this extent!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am genuinely concerned that we're seeing an exponential growth of self-aware articles that risks our community disappearing up its own arse in a pink glittery cloud of meta bullshit.

I think I should maybe have been clearer that this was part of the point of the final collapsible. There's not much room for subtext when the text itself is deliberately tough to chew on.

Operation Óvermeta used skips like 2747 and Fred, which subtly broke the fourth wall, to take a sledgehammer to that fourth wall in what they saw as a great victory. Now the whole set is starting to crumble and they're seeing the consequences of her actions. In-text, that's 200 Rogets bursting onto the scene, but I also reference the out-of-text stuff like 3999 and Magnus' 3000.

If that leaves a bad taste in your mouth due to its delivery, fair enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IMO, more playful and less self-indulgent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly don't think it's going to do as much damage as you think. I really don't think this warrants a "fuck this".

People have the same fear about meme related SCPs (internet memes) and even if they are being made, so far they haven't taken over this website. I don't think the meta will take over the website, as long as we keep our cool, which we have done in the past.

I understand your fear, and I respect that, but this really didn't warrant a "fuck this" in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fuck this.

I'm really sorry to be so blunt, but we're all about honest critique here, and I have to be honest about how much I dislike this.

I have a very low tolerance for meta stuff. I can probably handle it once per series. 2747 was a rare example I loved, mainly because it was so subtle that it never broke my suspension of disbelief. Andrew Swann's 001 proposal too, because the meta element fits thematically as part of an origin story.

But between 2747, and LordStonefish's 3000 proposal, and Rimple's own userpage, and this, I am genuinely concerned that we're seeing an exponential growth of self-aware articles that risks our community disappearing up its own arse in a pink glittery cloud of meta bullshit.

Look, the meta stuff is fun and all, and what you've written here is genuinely clever, and it's a new way to think about the narrative universe, and I admire it intellectually. But if we all start breaking boundaries for novelty value, we'll end up in a horrible boundary-breaking arms race, and soon enough there won't be any boundaries left, and we'll all be left sitting in a heap of wreckage feeling real stupid about ourselves. I unironically believe this.

I don't like getting this vehement, but I have to make clear the intensity of my emotions. Initial outburst aside, I hope I haven't been too rude or personal. The quality of prose here is excellent, and you should both be proud of it. However, not only do I despise the extreme meta element, I actively fear it, as I believe that it jeopardises the longevity of the SCP format as an institution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to be honest. I have no idea what any of this jargon meant, and I was more or less scratching my head in confusion as to what a majority of this meant. The jargon was too thick for me to understand, which is the main reason I downvoted.

My second reason being the metaness itself. I like me some metaness, don't get me wrong, but this just has too much 'wink wink nugde nudge's at the audience than I'm willing too enjoy or feel comfortable with. Meta stuff like this has to walk a fine line for me to enjoy it, and this doesn't walk it well for me. Something like Swann's proposal did what I feel is meta good in an SCP format. So It Was is a good example of meta being done good in a tale. I don't feel that this piece does meta well. A shame, really, because I wanted to like this. I really really wanted to like this. But I just can't.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I opened the first collapsible and just gave a tremendous shrug after a couple of seconds. I spent the rest of the article feeling like I was definitely missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gonna have to second this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But, it might also be "Rah-Jey" because the 'get' sound in some languages is 'jey'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I saw the name of it, I fell out of my seat. I was honestly not expecting something with that name to have been this good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the one I was also thinking too.
